Features bottom X percentile variance removed. example, 10 removes 10% features lowest variance. Default: 10. verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default: TRUE.

title: Filter Features by Low Variance — run_filtervariance
text: list containing: data Matrix data frame filtered data (class input x) features_removed Character vector removed feature names features_kept Character vector retained feature names variance_values Numeric vector variance values original features variance_threshold Numeric. variance value corresponding percentile cutoff n_features_before Integer. Number features filtering n_features_after Integer. Number features filtering n_features_removed Integer. Number features removed parameters List parameters used

title: Filter Features by Low Variance — run_filtervariance
text: Filter Variance: Features low variance across samples: Provide minimal discriminatory power classification Contribute little multivariate models (PCA, PLS-DA) May represent technical noise detection artifacts Increase computational burden without adding information Choosing Percentile: Conservative (5-10%): Remove least variable features Moderate (10-20%): Standard approach studies Aggressive (20-30%): high dimensionality concern default (10%) removes bottom 10% features variance, commonly used threshold metabolomics. Important Notes: Variance filtering applied normalization transformation, steps affect feature variance. variance calculation uses samples (including QCs present). Consider removing QC samples variance filtering artificially low variance. scaled data (auto Pareto), filter identifies features inherently low biological variation across study groups. high percentiles (>30%) risk removing biologically relevant features naturally stable expression levels.

title: Filter Features by Low Variance — run_filtervariance
text: Broadhurst, D.. (2025). QC:MXP Repeat Injection based Quality Control, Batch Correction, Exploration & Data Cleaning (Version 2.1) Zendono. doi:10.5281/zenodo.16824822 . Retrieved https://github.com/broadhurstdavid/QC-MXP.

title: Filter Features by Low Variance — run_filtervariance
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Filter Features by Low Variance — run_filtervariance
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data with varying levels of variance set.seed(123) n_samples <- 100 n_features <- 50  # Create features with different variance levels x <- matrix(nrow = n_samples, ncol = n_features) for (i in 1:n_features) {   # Variance increases with feature index   sd_val <- i / 2   x[, i] <- rnorm(n_samples, mean = 100, sd = sd_val) } colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:n_features)  # Remove bottom 10% (5 features with lowest variance) result1 <- run_filtervariance(x, percentile = 10)  # More aggressive filtering (bottom 20%) result2 <- run_filtervariance(x, percentile = 20) } # }

what: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: Computes pairwise fold changes (optionally log2 fold changes) feature (column) x across ordered group levels. Group means used basis comparison. log2 = TRUE data shifted values strictly positive log-transformation. function designed integrate seamlessly run_diff() plot_volcano.

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
code: run_foldchange(   x,   from = c("normalized", "corrected", "transformed", "scaled"),   metadata = NULL,   group = NULL,   arrange = NULL,   filter = NULL,   select = NULL,   sort = TRUE,   log2 = TRUE,   eps = 1e-08,   up = NULL,   down = NULL )

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: x data.frame, tibble, named matrix whose columns numeric features (variables) analysed. Column names may contain special characters. Character. Specifies data processing stage extract x run_DIpreprocess object. Options : "corrected" (drift/batch corrected), "normalized" (default), "transformed" (log/sqrt transformed), "scaled" (final auto/pareto scaled data). metadata data.frame, tibble, named matrix number rows x containing sample annotations. x run_DIpreprocess object, optional defaults internal metadata. group Character string naming column metadata contains grouping factor. x run_DIpreprocess object, optional defaults group column used step. arrange optional character vector listing levels group (filtering) desired comparison order. Fold changes computed pairwise combinations taken left--right: arrange[] / arrange[j] \(< j\). example, c("", "B", "C") yields A_vs_B, A_vs_C, B_vs_C. NULL (default), levels sorted alphanumerically. filter optional character vector group levels exclude analysis. x run_DIpreprocess object, defaults qc_types identified preprocessing. select optional character vector column names x include analysis. Default NULL uses columns x. sort Logical. TRUE (default), fold-change column output sorted highest lowest absolute fold change. log2 Logical. TRUE (default), log2 fold changes also computed. Data shifted prior log-transformation minimum value equals eps (see ), guaranteeing values strictly positive. eps small positive numeric value. log2 = TRUE, data shifted global minimum x (filtering selection) becomes eps. Default 1e-8. Numeric. Optional threshold -regulation. supplied, "sig" column added summary_table features fold_change >= labelled "Significant". Default NULL. Numeric. Optional threshold -regulation. supplied, features fold_change <= labelled "Significant" "sig" column summary_table. value compared raw fold change, log2. Default NULL.

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: named list containing: fc_table data.frame pairwise fold changes. Rows features; columns labelled "<level_i>_vs_<level_{+1}>". sort = TRUE column independently sorted (descending). log2fc_table data.frame log2 fold changes structure fc_table. NULL log2 = FALSE. shifted_data (possibly shifted) numeric data matrix used log2 computation. NULL log2 = FALSE. min_value global minimum analysis data shifting (numeric scalar). shift numeric shift \(\delta\) applied. 0 shift needed log2 = FALSE. group_means data.frame per-group, per-feature means (unshifted) one row per group level. comparisons character vector comparison labels, e.g. c("A_vs_B", "B_vs_C"). params list resolved parameter values reproducibility.

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: Fold change computation Group means computed per feature. \(k\) ordered levels \(g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_k\), fold changes computed pairwise combinations taken left--right arrange: \(\bar{x}_{g_i} / \bar{x}_{g_j}\) \(< j\), yielding \(k(k-1)/2\) comparisons (e.g., A_vs_B, A_vs_C, B_vs_C 3 groups). Log2 shifting log2 = TRUE, let \(m\) global minimum (filtered, selected) data matrix. shift \(\delta = \text{eps} - m\) added every value \(m \le 0\); otherwise shift applied (\(\delta = 0\)). Log2 fold changes computed shifted group means. Integration run_diff plot_volcano returned list exposes fc_table, log2fc_table, shifted_data consistent format plot_volcano() can consume directly.

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
code: ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 1 — basic usage with iris ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- data(iris) x_iris  <- iris[, 1:4] meta_iris <- data.frame(Species = iris$Species)  res <- run_foldchange(   x        = x_iris,   metadata = meta_iris,   group    = "Species" ) #> Error in match.names(clabs, names(xi)): names do not match previous names print(res$fc_table) #> Error: object 'res' not found print(res$log2fc_table) #> Error: object 'res' not found  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 2 — custom order, filter one level, select two features ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- res2 <- run_foldchange(   x        = x_iris,   metadata = meta_iris,   group    = "Species",   arrange  = c("virginica", "versicolor", "setosa"),   filter   = NULL,   select   = c("Sepal.Length", "Petal.Length") ) #> Error in match.names(clabs, names(xi)): names do not match previous names print(res2$comparisons) #> Error: object 'res2' not found print(res2$fc_table) #> Error: object 'res2' not found  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 3 — data with special characters in column names ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(1) n   <- 60 xsc <- data.frame(   `LPC 18:2`  = rnorm(n, 5, 1),   `PA O-28:1` = rnorm(n, 3, 0.5),   check.names = FALSE ) meta_sc <- data.frame(group = rep(c("Healthy", "Disease"), each = 30))  res3 <- run_foldchange(x = xsc, metadata = meta_sc, group = "group") print(res3$fc_table) #>     feature Disease_vs_Healthy #> 1  LPC 18:2             1.0099 #> 2 PA O-28:1             1.0005

what: Perform Binary Logistic Regression (Standard or Firth-Corrected) with Iterative Feature Combinations — run_logreg

text: robust wrapper glm (standard logistic regression) logistf (Firth's bias-reduced penalized-likelihood logistic regression) performs binary logistic regression. automatically handles column names spaces, computes advanced model fit metrics, maps specific reference categories, removes specified categories predictor variables, optionally iterates possible combinations independent variables. apply_firth = TRUE, Firth's correction applied selectively — models least one predictor-outcome cross-tabulation contains zero cell, primary condition warranting penalized likelihood. column output indicates whether Firth's correction applied model. Output formatted clean data frame.

title: Perform Binary Logistic Regression (Standard or Firth-Corrected) with Iterative Feature Combinations — run_logreg
code: run_logreg(   x,   y,   confounders = NULL,   indep = NULL,   ref,   ref_levels = NULL,   remove = NULL,   exclude = NULL,   iterate = FALSE,   add_vif = TRUE,   apply_firth = TRUE,   force_apply_firth = FALSE )

title: Perform Binary Logistic Regression (Standard or Firth-Corrected) with Iterative Feature Combinations — run_logreg
text: x data frame, matrix, tibble containing dataset. y string specifying valid, categorical column name x used dependent variable. confounders character vector valid column names x used confounding factors. Default NULL. indep character vector valid column names x used independent variables. Default NULL. ref string specifying valid category y used reference category. ref_levels list two-sided formulas mapping categorical predictor column names desired reference categories. left-hand side must valid column name x (quoted string), right-hand side must valid category column (e.g., list("Sex" ~ "Female", "District" ~ "North")). Default NULL. remove list two-sided formulas specifying categories drop predictor variables analysis. left-hand side must valid column name x (quoted string), right-hand side must valid category column removed. Multiple mappings may target column remove multiple categories (e.g., list("Sex" ~ "Unknown", "District" ~ "")). Default NULL. exclude character vector categories y drop analysis. Default NULL. iterate Logical; FALSE (default), combines confounders indep single model. TRUE, fits separate models possible combinations indep added confounders. add_vif Logical; TRUE (default), adds Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) values output. VIFs computed via car::vif(), returns GVIF categorical predictors standard VIF continuous predictors, consistent jamovi's output. Note VIF computed standard glm() fit even Firth's correction applied, car::vif() support logistf objects. apply_firth Logical; TRUE (default), Firth's bias-reduced penalized-likelihood logistic regression (logistf::logistf()) applied selectively — models least one predictor variable zero cell count cross-tabulation outcome. Models zero cells fitted using standard glm() regardless setting. logical column Firth Corrected added Metrics table (attached metrics iterate = FALSE) indicating whether Firth's correction applied model. force_apply_firth Logical; TRUE (default FALSE), overrides dynamic zero-cell detection apply_firth applies Firth's correction models regardless whether zero cells present. Ignored apply_firth = FALSE.

title: Perform Binary Logistic Regression (Standard or Firth-Corrected) with Iterative Feature Combinations — run_logreg
text: iterate = FALSE, returns data frame containing Predictor, Log Odds, Std Error, p-value, Significance, , 95\ McFadden R2, CoxSnell R2, Nagelkerke R2, Tjur R2, Firth Corrected) first row. Model metrics also attached attr(result, "model_metrics"). iterate = TRUE, returns named list containing: Tables named list result data frames, inline model metrics row 1. Metrics data frame comparing model metrics across combinations. filtered_Tables subset Tables excluding models non-intercept 95% CI Low values non-intercept 95% CI High values Inf. p_filtered_Tables subset filtered_Tables retaining models least one non-intercept p-value less .05. vif_filtered_Tables subset p_filtered_Tables retaining models non-NA VIF values less 5, ensuring violation multicollinearity.

title: Perform Binary Logistic Regression (Standard or Firth-Corrected) with Iterative Feature Combinations — run_logreg
text: iterate = TRUE, function generates possible combinations (power set) indep vector. example, indep = c("", "B", "C"), combinations evaluated alongside confounders : , B, C, +B, +C, B+C, +B+C. Firth's correction (apply_firth): Firth's penalized-likelihood method (logistf::logistf()) addresses monotone likelihood problem caused complete quasi-complete separation, arises predictor level perfectly predicts one outcome group — typically evidenced zero cell cross-tabulation. apply_firth = TRUE, function checks model's predictors outcome zero cells. found, logistf() used place glm() model. force_apply_firth = TRUE, check skipped models fitted logistf(). Firth Corrected column output records models received correction. Note Firth-corrected models report profile likelihood confidence intervals rather Wald intervals, consistent logistf's default behavior. Category removal (remove): Rows specified predictor variable equals given category dropped x model fitted. applied exclude factor releveling. Multiple formulas targeting column applied sequence, allowing several categories removed one variable. Aliased coefficients: standard glm() estimate coefficient due perfect quasi-complete separation, sets coefficient NA. summary() silently omits aliased rows confint.default() retains , causing row-count mismatch. run_logreg() detects automatically, aligns objects shared row names, emits warning naming every dropped aliased term. situation largely avoided apply_firth = TRUE since models routed logistf() instead. VIF computation: function uses car::vif() match jamovi's output. categorical predictors one degree freedom, car::vif() returns Generalized VIF (GVIF) degree-freedom-adjusted form \(GVIF^{1/(2\cdot df)}\). value stored VIF column raw GVIF (standard VIF continuous/binary predictors). car::vif() support logistf objects, VIF Firth-corrected models computed parallel standard glm() fit formula data. Significance codes: Significance column uses conventional scheme: *** \(p < .001\), ** \(p < .01\), * \(p < .05\), blank otherwise. Inline model metrics: Model-fit statistics (Deviance, AIC, BIC, McFadden R2, CoxSnell R2, Nagelkerke R2, Tjur R2, Firth Corrected) appended additional columns right VIF column. Values placed first row (aligned intercept); subsequent rows contain NA columns.

what: Perform Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — run_mixedmodel

text: Fits Linear Mixed-Effects Models (LMEMs) across features (columns) data matrix data frame, using metadata model covariates grouping structure. Results structured support downstream diagnostic effect visualizations including residuals vs. fitted, Q-Q plots, observed vs. predicted, effect plots, random effects caterpillar plots.

title: Perform Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — run_mixedmodel
code: run_mixedmodel(   x,   metadata,   formula,   subject_col = NULL,   group = NULL,   p_adjust_method = "BH",   exclude = NULL,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Perform Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — run_mixedmodel
text: x data frame, tibble, matrix numeric features modeled (e.g., metabolites, genes). Rows must correspond observations; columns features. Special characters column names supported. metadata data frame tibble number rows x, containing covariates, grouping variables, subject identifiers used model formula. formula character string specifying LMEM formula. response variable written .feature placeholder, substituted column x iteration (e.g., ".feature ~ time + (1 | subject_id)"). variables referenced formula (.feature) must present metadata. subject_col single character string specifying column name metadata subject identifiers used random effects. Defaults NULL, triggers automatic detection searching columns named "ID", "id", "SubjectID", "subject_id" order. warning issued match found subject_col remains NULL. group single character string specifying column name metadata encodes group membership (e.g., treatment arm, timepoint). Used structuring summaries downstream visualizations. Defaults NULL. p_adjust_method character string specifying p-value adjustment method combined fixed effects summary. Passed p.adjust. One "holm", "hochberg", "hommel", "bonferroni", "BH", "", "fdr", "none". Default "BH". exclude character vector column names present x, metadata, , removed analysis. Defaults NULL (exclusions). verbose Logical. TRUE, emits message feature processed. Default FALSE.

title: Perform Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — run_mixedmodel
text: named list class c("run_mixedmodel", "list") following top-level components: <feature_name> One entry per feature x. list : status Character string — "success" failure reason. model Fitted lmerModLmerTest object NULL. fixed_effects Data frame fixed effects. random_effects VarCorr. n_subjects Integer. n_obs Integer. convergence_warnings Character vector NULL. plot_data list plotting data: resid_vs_fitted Columns: fitted, residuals. qq Columns: theoretical, sample. obs_vs_pred Columns: observed, predicted. effects Effect estimates CIs. ranef_caterpillar Random effects summaries. combined_fixed_effects Data frame combining fixed effects successful models. parameters List input parameters reproducibility. processing_summary Summary n_features, n_success, n_failed, success_rate.

title: Perform Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — run_mixedmodel
text: function iterates columns x (exclusions), binding response variable combined analysis data frame alongside metadata. model formula constructed substituting literal token .feature backtick-quoted column name current feature. allows formulas arbitrary fixed random effects structures reused across features without modification. Models fit using lmerTest::lmer REML estimation, provides Satterthwaite-approximated degrees freedom p-values fixed effects. successfully fitted model, following diagnostic data extracted stored support downstream visualization: Residuals vs. Fitted: raw residuals fitted values. Q-Q plot: standardized residuals theoretical quantiles. Observed vs. Predicted: response vector alongside fitted values. Effect plots: fixed effect estimates 95\ Random effects (caterpillar): conditional modes conditional standard deviations per random effect grouping factor. Adjusted p-values Combined_Fixed_Effects_Summary computed separately within unique fixed effect term across features, using method specified p_adjust_method.

title: Perform Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — run_mixedmodel
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Perform Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — run_mixedmodel
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(42) n_subj  <- 20 n_times <- 2 n       <- n_subj * n_times  metadata <- data.frame(   subject_id = factor(rep(seq_len(n_subj), each = n_times)),   time       = rep(c("pre", "post"), n_subj),   age        = rep(round(runif(n_subj, 25, 60)), each = n_times),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  x <- data.frame(   glucose   = rnorm(n, mean = ifelse(metadata$time == "post", 5.5, 5.0), sd = 0.5),   insulin   = rnorm(n, mean = ifelse(metadata$time == "post", 90,  80 ), sd = 10),   `PA O-28:1` = rnorm(n, mean = 1.2, sd = 0.3),   check.names = FALSE )  result <- run_mixedmodel(   x        = x,   metadata = metadata,   formula  = ".feature ~ time + age + (1 | subject_id)",   group    = "time" )  print(result) summary(result) head(result$combined_fixed_effects) } # }

code: # 1. Move row names to the first column (default) run_move_names(mtcars[1:5, 1:3]) #>           row_names  mpg cyl disp #> 1         Mazda RX4 21.0   6  160 #> 2     Mazda RX4 Wag 21.0   6  160 #> 3        Datsun 710 22.8   4  108 #> 4    Hornet 4 Drive 21.4   6  258 #> 5 Hornet Sportabout 18.7   8  360  # 2. Move row names to a specific column index run_move_names(mtcars[1:5, 1:3], col_index = 2) #>    mpg         row_names cyl disp #> 1 21.0         Mazda RX4   6  160 #> 2 21.0     Mazda RX4 Wag   6  160 #> 3 22.8        Datsun 710   4  108 #> 4 21.4    Hornet 4 Drive   6  258 #> 5 18.7 Hornet Sportabout   8  360  # 3. Move column names to the first row # Note: col_index must be NULL to process row_index per requirements run_move_names(mtcars[1:5, 1:3], col_index = NULL, row_index = 1) #>                     V1  V2   V3 #> cn                 mpg cyl disp #> Mazda RX4           21   6  160 #> Mazda RX4 Wag       21   6  160 #> Datsun 710        22.8   4  108 #> Hornet 4 Drive    21.4   6  258 #> Hornet Sportabout 18.7   8  360  # 4. Demonstrate priority warning run_move_names(mtcars[1:5, 1:3], col_index = 1, row_index = 1) #> Warning: Ambiguity detected: Both 'col_index' and 'row_index' provided. Prioritizing movement of row names to column. 'row_index' will be ignored. #>           row_names  mpg cyl disp #> 1         Mazda RX4 21.0   6  160 #> 2     Mazda RX4 Wag 21.0   6  160 #> 3        Datsun 710 22.8   4  108 #> 4    Hornet 4 Drive 21.4   6  258 #> 5 Hornet Sportabout 18.7   8  360

what: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
title: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
text: Replaces missing values (NAs) using either simple deterministic method (fraction minimum), statistical imputation method (quantile regression imputation left-censored data, QRILC), method available mice (Multiple Imputation Chained Equations).

title: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
code: run_mvimpute(   x,   method = 0.2,   positive_only = TRUE,   tune_sigma = 1,   m = 5,   maxit = 5,   seed = NA,   verbose = TRUE,   ... )

title: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
text: x Matrix data frame. Numeric data samples rows features columns. Missing values represented NA. method Numeric character. Controls imputation strategy: Numeric — fraction smallest observed value per feature used deterministic imputation (e.g., 0.2 = 1/5th smallest value). default (positive_only = TRUE), minimum computed strictly positive values , standard MNAR convention metabolomics. Set positive_only = FALSE use global minimum including zeros negative values. Recommended range: 0.01–0.5. Values > 1 trigger warning. "quantileregression" — uses QRILC algorithm imputeLCMD package. Assumes Missing Random (MNAR) data. Controlled tune_sigma. mice method string — e.g., "pmm", "norm", "norm.boot", "norm.nob", "mean", "2l.norm", "rf", "cart", etc. Delegates mice mice package. Controlled m, maxit, seed, .... Default: 0.2 (1/5th minimum, deterministic). positive_only Logical. used method numeric. TRUE (default), per-feature minimum computed strictly positive values (zeros negatives excluded), standard practice MNAR imputation metabolomics missing values represent abundances detection limit. FALSE, global minimum across observed (non-NA) values used, may appropriate features can legitimately zero negative (e.g., log-transformed mean-centred data). effect method "quantileregression" mice method string. tune_sigma Numeric. used method = "quantileregression". Controls standard deviation left-censored missing value distribution. Smaller values (e.g., 0.5) assume tighter distribution; larger values (e.g., 2) allow spread. Default: 1. m Integer. used method routes mice. Number multiple imputations. first completed dataset used imputed result. Default: 5. maxit Integer. used method routes mice. Number iterations chained equations algorithm. Default: 5. seed Integer NA. used method routes mice. Random seed passed mice reproducibility. Default: NA (seed set). verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default: TRUE. ... Additional arguments passed mice method routes mice engine. effect numeric "quantileregression" methods. notable argument predictorMatrix: default, run_mvimpute builds one automatically via quickpred avoid failures caused constant collinear features, common wide metabolomics/metagenomics data. Supply predictorMatrix via ... override .

title: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
text: list following elements: data Matrix data frame imputed values (class input x). n_missing_before Integer. Number missing values imputation. n_missing_after Integer. Number missing values imputation (0). imputed_summary Data frame per-feature imputation statistics. parameters List parameters used, including method, positive_only, tune_sigma (QRILC ), m/maxit/seed (mice ). method_used Character string describing imputation method applied.

title: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
text: Missing Value Imputation Strategies: Deterministic (fraction minimum): method numeric, missing value feature replaced fraction per-feature minimum, multiplied specified fraction. minimum determined positive_only: positive_only = TRUE (default): uses smallest strictly positive observed value per feature, excluding zeros negatives. standard metabolomics values detection limit (MNAR — Missing Random). positive_only = FALSE: uses global minimum across observed (non-NA) values, including zeros negatives. Suitable data log-transformed mean-centred. valid minimum can found (e.g., feature entirely NA , positive_only = TRUE, positive values), fallback 1e-9 used. Quantile Regression (QRILC): method = "quantileregression", uses Quantile Regression Imputation Left-Censored data algorithm imputeLCMD package. Models missing values left-censored distribution, controlled tune_sigma. statistically principled simple fraction--minimum MNAR data. Requires imputeLCMD: BiocManager::install("imputeLCMD"). mice (Multiple Imputation Chained Equations): method recognised mice method string, mice called internally feature matrix. first m completed datasets extracted via mice::complete() returned. Supported mice methods include (non-exhaustive): "pmm" (predictive mean matching), "norm", "norm.boot", "norm.nob", "mean", "rf" (random forest), "cart", "2l.norm", "midastouch", "sample". See mice full list. Requires mice: install.packages("mice"). default, run_mvimpute builds predictor matrix automatically via quickpred guard common failure mice removes predictors due constant collinear columns (frequent wide, sparse metabolomics/metagenomics data). Supply custom predictorMatrix via ... override. Additional arguments mice can also passed via ....

title: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
text: Van Buuren, S., & Groothuis-Oudshoorn, K. (2011). mice: Multivariate Imputation Chained Equations R. Journal Statistical Software, 45(3), 1–67. doi:10.18637/jss.v045.i03 Wei, R., Wang, J., Su, M., Jia, E., Chen, S., Chen, T., & Ni, Y. (2018). Missing Value Imputation Approach Mass Spectrometry-based Metabolomics Data. Scientific Reports, 8(1), 663. doi:10.1038/s41598-017-19120-0 Lazar, C., Gatto, L., Ferro, M., Bruley, C., & Burger, T. (2016). Accounting Multiple Natures Missing Values Label-Free Quantitative Proteomics Data Sets Compare Imputation Strategies. Journal Proteome Research, 15(4), 1116–1125. doi:10.1021/acs.jproteome.5b00981

title: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Impute Missing Values in Metabolomics Data — run_mvimpute
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data with missing values set.seed(123) x <- matrix(abs(rnorm(100 * 50, mean = 100)), nrow = 100, ncol = 50) x[sample(length(x), 200)] <- NA colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:50)  # 1. Deterministic imputation (1/5th of minimum positive value per feature) result1 <- run_mvimpute(x, method = 0.2)  # 2. Deterministic imputation using global minimum (including zeros/negatives) result2 <- run_mvimpute(x, method = 0.2, positive_only = FALSE)  # 3. Quantile regression imputation (QRILC) result3 <- run_mvimpute(x, method = "quantileregression", tune_sigma = 1)  # 4. Predictive mean matching via mice result4 <- run_mvimpute(x, method = "pmm", m = 5, maxit = 5, seed = 42)  # 5. Random forest imputation via mice result5 <- run_mvimpute(x, method = "rf", m = 5, maxit = 5, seed = 42)  # 6. Passing additional mice arguments via ... result6 <- run_mvimpute(x, method = "norm.boot", m = 10, printFlag = FALSE) } # }

title: Normalize Metabolomics Data — run_normalize
text: Applies various normalization methods account dilution effects sample--sample variation metabolomics data.

title: Normalize Metabolomics Data — run_normalize
code: run_normalize(   x,   metadata,   method = "sum",   factor_col = "Normalization",   ref_sample = NULL,   group_sample = "QC",   qc_normalize = "median",   groups = "Group",   qc_types = c("QC", "SQC", "EQC"),   reference_method = "mean",   sample_id_col = "Sample",   verbose = TRUE )

title: Normalize Metabolomics Data — run_normalize
text: x Matrix data frame. Numeric data samples rows features columns. metadata Data frame. Sample metadata number rows equal nrow(x). May contain normalization factors, group labels, QC sample identifiers. method Character numeric vector. Normalization method apply. Options: "sum": Total sum normalization "median": Median normalization "specific_factor": Use values metadata column (specify via factor_col) "pqn_global": Probabilistic Quotient Normalization using global median "pqn_reference": PQN using specific reference sample (specify via ref_sample) "pqn_group": PQN using pooled QC samples reference "quantile": Quantile normalization "col_rel_abundance": Relative abundance per column (feature); value divided column sum feature sums 1 across samples "row_rel_abundance": Relative abundance per row (sample); value divided row sum sample sums 1 across features Numeric vector: Custom normalization factors (length must equal nrow(x) excluding QCs) factor_col Character. Name column metadata containing normalization factors method = "specific_factor". Default: "Normalization". ref_sample Character. Sample name use reference method = "pqn_reference". Must match value sample identifier column metadata. Default: NULL. group_sample Character. Group label method = "pqn_group". Uses samples group label reference. Default: "QC". qc_normalize Character. normalize QC samples using specific factors. Options: "mean", "median", "none". Default: "median". groups Character. Name column metadata containing group labels. Required PQN QC identification. Default: "Group". qc_types Character vector. Group labels identifying QC samples. Default: c("QC", "SQC", "EQC"). reference_method Character. Method computing reference quantile normalization. Options: "mean", "median". Default: "mean". sample_id_col Character. Name column metadata containing sample identifiers matching ref_sample. Default: "Sample". verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default: TRUE.

title: Normalize Metabolomics Data — run_normalize
text: list containing: data Matrix data frame normalized data (class input x) normalization_factors Numeric vector factors used normalization method_used Character describing normalization method applied parameters List parameters used

title: Normalize Metabolomics Data — run_normalize
text: Normalization Methods: sum: Divides sample total sum. Assumes similar total metabolite abundance across samples. median: Divides sample median value. robust outliers sum. specific_factor: Uses externally measured factors (e.g., osmolality urine, protein concentration plasma) metadata column. QC samples normalized separately using mean/median biological sample factors. pqn_global: Probabilistic Quotient Normalization using global median spectrum reference. Robust method accounts dilution effects. pqn_reference: PQN using specific reference sample. Useful one sample represents "ideal" composition. pqn_group: PQN using pooled QC samples reference. Recommended metabolomics data regular QC injections. quantile: Forces samples identical distributions. strong normalization may remove biological variation - use caution. col_rel_abundance: Computes relative abundance per feature (column). value divided sum column, feature's values sum 1 across samples. Useful comparing contribution feature across samples. Note normalization factors returned column sums. row_rel_abundance: Computes relative abundance per sample (row). value divided sum row, sample's values sum 1 across features. Equivalent "sum" normalization expressed proportions. Useful compositional data analysis. Note normalization factors returned row sums. Custom Normalization Factors: can provide numeric vector method custom normalization factors. vector length must equal number biological samples (excluding QCs). QC samples normalized using mean median factors.

title: Normalize Metabolomics Data — run_normalize
text: Dieterle, F., Ross, ., Schlotterbeck, G., & Senn, H. (2006). Probabilistic Quotient Normalization Robust Method Account Dilution Complex Biological Mixtures. Analytical Chemistry, 78(13), 4281-4290. doi:10.1021/ac051632c Jankevics , Lloyd GR, Weber RJM (2025). pmp: Peak Matrix Processing signal batch correction metabolomics datasets. doi:10.18129/B9.bioc.pmp , R package version 1.20.0, https://bioconductor.org/packages/pmp.

title: Normalize Metabolomics Data — run_normalize
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Normalize Metabolomics Data — run_normalize
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data set.seed(123) x <- matrix(abs(rnorm(100 * 50, mean = 100, sd = 20)),             nrow = 100, ncol = 50) colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:50)  metadata <- data.frame(   Sample = paste0("S", 1:100),   Group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment", "QC"), c(40, 40, 20)),   Normalization = runif(100, 0.8, 1.2) )  # Sum normalization result1 <- run_normalize(x, metadata, method = "sum")  # PQN with QC reference result2 <- run_normalize(x, metadata, method = "pqn_group")  # Specific factors from metadata result3 <- run_normalize(x, metadata, method = "specific_factor",                          factor_col = "Normalization")  # Relative abundance per feature (column) result4 <- run_normalize(x, metadata, method = "col_rel_abundance")  # Relative abundance per sample (row) result5 <- run_normalize(x, metadata, method = "row_rel_abundance") } # }

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
text: Performs Principal Component Analysis (PCA) preprocessed metabolomics data. Optionally applies scaling transformation PCA input data pre-scaled. Supports sample exclusion group.

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
code: run_pca(   x,   metadata,   scale_method = NULL,   transform_method = NULL,   group = "Group",   exclude = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
text: x Matrix data frame. Numeric data samples rows features columns. Can output run_scale() raw data (scaled scale_method specified). metadata Data frame. Sample metadata number rows equal nrow(x). Must contain columns grouping, batch information, sample identifiers. scale_method Character NULL. Scaling method apply PCA x already scaled. Options: "mean", "auto", "pareto". NULL (default) x run_scale(), warning issued PCA proceeds without additional scaling. transform_method Character NULL. Transformation method apply scaling PCA. Options: "log2", "log10", "sqrt", "cbrt", "vsn", "glog". Default: NULL (transformation). group Character. Name column metadata containing group labels sample exclusion. Required exclude specified. Default: "Group". exclude Character vector NULL. Group labels (group column) exclude PCA analysis. example, c("QC", "Blank") excludes samples group labels. Default: NULL (include samples). verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default: TRUE.

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
text: list containing: scores Matrix PC scores (samples × PCs) loadings Matrix PC loadings (features × PCs) variance_explained Numeric vector variance explained (%) per PC eigenvalues Numeric vector eigenvalues per PC cumulative_variance Numeric vector cumulative variance explained (%) per PC center_values Numeric vector centering values used () scale_values Numeric vector scaling values used () pca_object Original prcomp object stats::prcomp data_used Matrix data used PCA (transformation/scaling) metadata Data frame metadata aligned PCA scores n_samples Integer. Number samples used PCA n_samples_excluded Integer. Number samples excluded n_features Integer. Number features n_pcs Integer. Number PCs computed excluded_groups Character vector excluded group labels preprocessing List documenting preprocessing steps applied parameters List parameters used

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
text: Sample Exclusion: exclude parameter allows removal specific sample groups PCA: Common use: Exclude QC samples (exclude = c("QC", "SQC", "EQC")) Multiple groups can excluded: exclude = c("QC", "Blank", "Failed") Exclusion performed preprocessing steps Excluded samples included transformation/scaling calculations Input Data Requirements: function accepts data two forms: Pre-scaled data run_scale(): PCA performed directly without additional preprocessing. recommended workflow. Raw data: scale_method specified, function first apply optional transformation (via transform_method), scaling (via scale_method), performing PCA. PCA Methodology: PCA performed using singular value decomposition (SVD) via stats::prcomp(). function assumes data appropriately preprocessed (normalized, transformed, scaled) analysis. PCA performed without additional centering scaling (center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE) applied scaling step. Interpretation: PC Scores: Coordinates samples principal component space. Used visualization identifying patterns/clusters. PC Loadings: Contribution feature principal component. Features high absolute loadings drive separation along PC. Variance Explained: Percentage total variance captured PC. First PCs typically capture variance metabolomics data. Eigenvalues: Variance captured PC original units. Eigenvalue > 1 (Kaiser criterion) often used determine significant PCs. Scaling Recommendations: Auto-scaling ("auto"): Recommended PCA. Gives equal weight features regardless magnitude. Pareto-scaling ("pareto"): Alternative partially preserves variance structure. Can useful feature magnitudes carry biological meaning. Mean-centering ("mean"): centers data. Use features already comparable scales.

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
text: Becker, R.., Chambers, J.M., & Wilks, .R. (1988). New S Language. Wadsworth & Brooks/Cole. doi:10.1201/9781351074988 Mardia, K.V., Kent, J.T., & Bibby, J.M. (1979). Multivariate Analysis. London: Academic Press. Venables, W.N., & Ripley, B.D. (2002). Modern Applied Statistics S. Springer-Verlag. van den Berg, R.., Hoefsloot, H.C., Westerhuis, J.., Smilde, .K., & van der Werf, M.J. (2006). Centering, scaling, transformations: improving biological information content metabolomics data. BMC Genomics, 7, 142. doi:10.1186/1471-2164-7-142

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data set.seed(123) x <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 50, mean = 100, sd = 20),             nrow = 100, ncol = 50) colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:50) rownames(x) <- paste0("Sample", 1:100)  metadata <- data.frame(   Sample = paste0("Sample", 1:100),   Group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment", "QC"), c(40, 40, 20)),   Batch = rep(1:4, each = 25),   Time = rep(c(0, 6, 12, 24), 25) )  # PCA on pre-scaled data (recommended) scaled_result <- run_scale(x, method = "auto") pca_result <- run_pca(scaled_result$data, metadata)  # PCA excluding QC samples pca_result2 <- run_pca(scaled_result$data, metadata,                         group = "Group",                        exclude = "QC")  # PCA with automatic scaling, excluding multiple groups pca_result3 <- run_pca(x, metadata,                         scale_method = "auto",                        group = "Group",                        exclude = c("QC", "Blank"))  # PCA with transformation and scaling pca_result4 <- run_pca(x, metadata,                         transform_method = "log2",                        scale_method = "pareto",                        group = "Group",                        exclude = "QC")  # View results print(pca_result) summary(pca_result) } # }

what: Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) with Optional PERMANOVA and Post-hoc — run_pcoa
title: Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) with Optional PERMANOVA and Post-hoc — run_pcoa
text: Performs Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA), also known metric multidimensional scaling (MDS), sequential wrapper around vegdist pcoa. Optionally runs Permutational Multivariate Analysis Variance (PERMANOVA) via adonis2 dissimilarity matrix. Can also perform pairwise post-hoc testing using OmicFlow::pairwise_adonis. analyses share single dissimilarity matrix method applied exactly .

title: Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) with Optional PERMANOVA and Post-hoc — run_pcoa
text: PERMANOVA (Anderson, 2001; McArdle & Anderson, 2001) partitions multivariate variance using distance matrix tests significance permuting rows. robust non-normality sensitive heterogeneous within-group dispersions (Warton et al., 2012); use alongside test homogeneity dispersions (vegan::betadisper) groups differ spread. Key output fields adonis2: R²: proportion total variance explained grouping factor. F: pseudo-F statistic. Pr(>F): permutation p-value.

title: Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) with Optional PERMANOVA and Post-hoc — run_pcoa
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) with Optional PERMANOVA and Post-hoc — run_pcoa
code: ## ── Example 1: Bray–Curtis PCoA on the dune dataset ────────────────────── if (requireNamespace("vegan", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("ape",   quietly = TRUE)) {    data(dune,     package = "vegan")   data(dune.env, package = "vegan")    result <- run_pcoa(dune, method = "bray")   print(result)   summary(result)   head(result$scores[, c("PC1", "PC2")])   result$variance_explained[1:3] } #> Warning: 5 negative eigenvalue(s) detected in the PCoA decomposition. #>   This is common with non-Euclidean dissimilarity indices (e.g., Bray-Curtis). #>   Consider correction = "lingoes" or "cailliez" for a fully Euclidean representation. #> Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) #> ----------------------------------------  #> Dissimilarity method : bray  #> Correction           : none  #> Observations         : 20  #> Axes retained        : 14  #>  #> Variance explained (first 5 axes): #>   PC1: 37.36% #>   PC2: 22.26% #>   PC3: 10.05% #>   PC4: 8.32% #>   PC5: 6.08% #>  #> =======================================================  #> Principal Coordinate Analysis — Summary #> =======================================================  #>  #> Call: #>    run_pcoa(x = dune, method = "bray")  #>  #> Dissimilarity method :  bray  #> Correction           :  none  #> Observations (n)     :  20  #> Total axes           :  14  #>  #> Eigenvalues and variance explained (first 5 axes): #>  Axis Eigenvalue Variance_pct Cumulative_pct #>   PC1    1.71627       37.359         37.359 #>   PC2    1.02240       22.255         59.615 #>   PC3    0.46146       10.045         69.660 #>   PC4    0.38225        8.321         77.980 #>   PC5    0.27913        6.076         84.057 #>  #> =======================================================  #>      PC1      PC2      PC3  #> 37.35930 22.25533 10.04505   ## ── Example 2: PCoA + PERMANOVA ─────────────────────────────────────────── if (requireNamespace("vegan", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("ape",   quietly = TRUE)) {    data(dune,     package = "vegan")   data(dune.env, package = "vegan")    result_perm <- run_pcoa(     x        = dune,     method   = "bray",     metadata = dune.env,     rhs      = "Management",     perms    = 999   )    # Extract PERMANOVA results individually   result_perm$permanova$R2   result_perm$permanova$p_value   result_perm$permanova$table } #> Warning: 5 negative eigenvalue(s) detected in the PCoA decomposition. #>   This is common with non-Euclidean dissimilarity indices (e.g., Bray-Curtis). #>   Consider correction = "lingoes" or "cailliez" for a fully Euclidean representation. #> Warning: number of items to replace is not a multiple of replacement length #>            Df SumOfSqs        R2        F Pr(>F) #> Management  3 1.468592 0.3416107 2.767243  0.002 #> Residual   16 2.830430 0.6583893       NA     NA #> Total      19 4.299022 1.0000000       NA     NA  ## ── Example 3: Lingoes correction ───────────────────────────────────────── if (requireNamespace("vegan", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("ape",   quietly = TRUE)) {    data(dune, package = "vegan")   result_ling <- run_pcoa(dune, method = "bray", correction = "lingoes")   result_ling$variance_explained[1:3] } #>       PC1       PC2       PC3  #> 29.538396 18.233837  9.095053   ## ── Example 4: Precomputed dist + PERMANOVA ─────────────────────────────── if (requireNamespace("vegan", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("ape",   quietly = TRUE)) {    data(dune,     package = "vegan")   data(dune.env, package = "vegan")   d <- vegan::vegdist(dune, method = "jaccard")    result_dist <- run_pcoa(     x        = d,     metadata = dune.env,     rhs      = "Management",     perms    = 999   )   result_dist$method          # "user-supplied dist"   result_dist$permanova$R2 } #> Warning: 1 negative eigenvalue(s) detected in the PCoA decomposition. #>   This is common with non-Euclidean dissimilarity indices (e.g., Bray-Curtis). #>   Consider correction = "lingoes" or "cailliez" for a fully Euclidean representation. #> Warning: number of items to replace is not a multiple of replacement length #> [1] 0.2943778

what: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
title: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
text: Performs Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA), Orthogonal PLS-DA (OPLS-DA), sparse PLS-DA (sPLS-DA) preprocessed metabolomics data. Optionally applies scaling transformation analysis input data pre-scaled. Designed match structure run_pca().

title: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
code: run_pls(   x,   metadata,   method = "oplsda",   group = "Group",   exclude = NULL,   scale_method = NULL,   transform_method = NULL,   ncomp = 2,   orthoI = NA,   crossvalI = 10,   permI = 20,   keepX = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
text: x Matrix data frame. Numeric data samples rows features columns. Can output run_scale(), raw data (scaled scale_method, run_DIpreprocess. specified). metadata Data frame. Sample metadata number rows equal nrow(x). Ignored, even supplied, x run_DIpreprocess class. Must contain columns grouping sample identifiers. method Character. PLS method use. Options "oplsda" (default), "plsda", "splsda". group Character. Name column metadata containing group labels classification sample exclusion. Required parameter. Default: "Group". exclude Character vector NULL. Group labels (group column) exclude analysis. example, c("QC", "Blank") excludes samples group labels. Default: NULL (include samples). scale_method Character NULL. Scaling method apply PLS x already scaled. Options: "mean", "auto", "pareto". NULL (default), function assumes data appropriately scaled. Pareto scaling generally recommended PLS methods. transform_method Character NULL. Transformation method apply scaling PLS. Options: "log2", "log10", "sqrt", "cbrt", "vsn", "glog". Default: NULL (transformation). ncomp Integer. Number predictive components compute PLS-DA sPLS-DA. Default: 2. orthoI Integer NA. Number orthogonal components OPLS-DA. NA (default), optimal number determined automatically cross-validation. crossvalI Integer. Number cross-validation folds. Default: 10. permI Integer. Number permutations model validation. Default: 20. keepX Integer vector. sPLS-DA : specifies number variables keep component. Default: NULL (automatic selection keeps ). verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default: TRUE.

title: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
text: list containing: scores Matrix scores (samples × components), aliased PC1, PC2... plot_score compatibility loadings Matrix feature loadings (features × components) variance_explained Numeric vector variance explained (%) per component vip_scores Numeric vector Variable Importance Projection (VIP) scores (PLS-DA/OPLS-DA ) pls_object Original model object ropls mixOmics data_used Matrix data used PLS (transformation/scaling) metadata Data frame metadata aligned scores n_samples Integer. Number samples used n_samples_excluded Integer. Number samples excluded n_features Integer. Number features n_pcs Integer. Number components computed excluded_groups Character vector excluded group labels preprocessing List documenting preprocessing steps applied parameters List parameters used

title: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
text: Compatibility: output function specifically designed compatible plot_score(). Component names scores matrix aliased PC1, PC2, etc., allow seamless plotting. OPLS-DA, "PC1" represents Predictive Component, "PC2" represents first Orthogonal Component. Methods: oplsda: Orthogonal PLS-DA separates predictive variation (correlated class) orthogonal variation (uncorrelated). Excellent biomarker discovery. Utilizes ropls::opls(). plsda: Standard PLS-DA. Maximizes covariance features class labels. Utilizes ropls::opls(). splsda: Sparse PLS-DA incorporates L1 penalization perform feature selection model building. Utilizes mixOmics::splsda(). Sample Exclusion: QC samples typically excluded discriminant analysis model building. Use exclude parameter (e.g., exclude = "QC") drop prior scaling fitting.

title: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
text: Thevenot, E. ., Roux, ., Xu, Y., Ezan, E., & Junot, C. (2015). Analysis Human Adult Urinary Metabolome Variations Age, Body Mass Index, Gender Implementing Comprehensive Workflow Univariate OPLS Statistical Analyses. Journal Proteome Research, 14(8), 3322-3335. Rohart F, Gautier B, Singh , Lê Cao K-. (2017) mixOmics: R package 'omics feature selection multiple data integration. PLoS Comput Biol 13(11): e1005752.

title: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Perform Partial Least Squares (PLS) Analysis — run_pls
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data set.seed(123) x <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 50, mean = 100, sd = 20), nrow = 100, ncol = 50) colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:50) rownames(x) <- paste0("Sample", 1:100)  metadata <- data.frame(   Sample = paste0("Sample", 1:100),   Group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment", "QC"), c(40, 40, 20)) )  # Run PLS-DA excluding QC samples, with automatic pareto scaling pls_result <- run_pls(x, metadata,                        method = "plsda",                       scale_method = "pareto",                       group = "Group",                       exclude = "QC")  # Visualize with plot_score plot_score(pls_result, color_by = "Group", points_from = "Sample")  # Run OPLS-DA opls_result <- run_pls(x, metadata,                         method = "oplsda",                        scale_method = "pareto",                        group = "Group",                        exclude = "QC") } # }

what: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: Randomly samples rows data frame, matrix, elements vector, optional group-stratified sampling. Serves structured wrapper around slice_sample, extending flexible per-group control (joint sequential/independent stratification), reproducible seeding, strict input validation suitable biological clinical research workflows.

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
code: run_randomize(   x,   n = NULL,   p = NULL,   per_group = NULL,   joint = TRUE,   joint_behaviour = "cascading",   replace = FALSE,   seed = 123L )

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: x data frame, tibble, matrix, atomic vector. Column names special characters supported. Matrices coerced data frames internally result recast matrix return. n single positive integer specifying number rows (elements, x vector) return. Defaults NULL. Mutually exclusive p; supplied, n takes priority warning issued. per_group supplied without explicit n/p inside list, top-level value used fallback. p single numeric value [0, 1] specifying proportion rows (elements) return. Defaults NULL (equivalent p = 1, .e., rows shuffled). Mutually exclusive n. per_group supplied without explicit n/p inside list, top-level value used fallback. per_group optional list per-group sampling specs controlling stratified sampling. element must follow one two forms: Single column: list("ColumnName", n = <integer>) list("ColumnName", p = <number>) Multiple columns (joint independent): list(c("Col1", "Col2"), n = <integer>) list(c("Col1", "Col2"), p = <number>) list(c("Col1", "Col2"), n = c(2, 3)) (per-column values, joint = FALSE + joint_behaviour = "independent" ) n/p inside list element optional. omitted, top-level n p argument used fallback (top-level n takes priority top-level p provided). n p supplied inside list element, n takes priority warning issued. Requires x data frame matrix. Grouping columns must categorical (character, factor, logical). See joint joint_behaviour multiple columns handled. joint single logical value. Defaults TRUE. TRUE: columns listed per_group entry treated simultaneously — rows grouped every unique combination levels across columns (via interaction), n/p enforced combination. example, cyl gear, temporary group key "6:4" "8:3" created internally discarded sampling. FALSE: joint_behaviour argument controls whether columns handled cascading independent fashion. Ignored single column listed per_group entry. joint_behaviour character string, either "cascading" ("c") "independent" (""). applies joint = FALSE per_group entry lists multiple columns. Defaults "cascading". "cascading": columns applied sequentially order listed. data first grouped column 1 sampled; reduced result grouped column 2 sampled , . step's n/p applied whatever rows remain previous step, can aggressively reduce output n small. "independent": column sampled independently original x. results combined taking union selected row indices, row qualifies chosen grouping columns. per-column n/p vectors supplied (e.g., n = c(2, 3)), value corresponds positionally column. Vectors must exceed number columns length. replace single logical value. FALSE (default), sampling performed without replacement. TRUE, rows elements may repeated output. seed single integer passed set.seed reproducibility. Defaults 123L. Supply NA skip seeding (non-reproducible runs).

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: object class x: Data frame / tibble: data frame tibble sampled rows, preserving columns classes. Row names dropped (consistent slice_sample behaviour). Matrix: matrix sampled rows, preserving column names storage mode. Vector: atomic vector sampled elements, preserving names present.

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: run_randomize() operates three mutually exclusive top-level modes, resolved following order priority: Per-group stratified sampling (per_group NULL): Rows sampled within groups defined column(s) per_group entry. joint joint_behaviour arguments control multiple columns handled (see ). Fixed-count sampling (n supplied, per_group =     NULL): Exactly n rows returned. Setting n equal nrow(x) returns rows new random order. Proportional sampling (p supplied, n p omitted): fraction p rows returned. Omitting defaults p = 1 (full shuffle). p = 0 returns zero-row object class.

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: element per_group bundles one grouping columns optional sampling quantity:
code: # Single column, explicit n per_group = list(   list("Species", n = 10) )  # Two columns, joint (default), explicit n shared across all combinations per_group = list(   list(c("cyl", "gear"), n = 2) )  # Two columns, independent, per-column n vector per_group = list(   list(c("cyl", "gear"), n = c(2, 3)) )  # Omit n/p to fall back on the top-level n or p argument per_group = list(   list("Species") )

title: Reduce Multiple Data Frames by Common or Unique Column/Row Names — run_reduce
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Reduce Multiple Data Frames by Common or Unique Column/Row Names — run_reduce
code: ## --- Example data --------------------------------------------------- df1 <- data.frame(a = 1:3, b = 4:6,  c = 7:9) df2 <- data.frame(a = 10:12, b = 13:15, d = 16:18) df3 <- data.frame(a = 19:21, b = 22:24, e = 25:27)  ## 1. Return columns common to ALL data frames (default) result_common <- run_reduce(df1, df2, df3) result_common$data          # columns: a, b #>    a  b #> 1  1  4 #> 2  2  5 #> 3  3  6 #> 4 10 13 #> 5 11 14 #> 6 12 15 #> 7 19 22 #> 8 20 23 #> 9 21 24 result_common$summary       # columns dropped per frame #> $df1 #> [1] "c" #>  #> $df2 #> [1] "d" #>  #> $df3 #> [1] "e" #>   ## 2. Return columns from ANY data frame (union, fill with NA) result_union <- run_reduce(df1, df2, df3, return_common = FALSE) result_union$data           # columns: a, b, c, d, e  (NAs where absent) #>    a  b  c  d  e #> 1  1  4  7 NA NA #> 2  2  5  8 NA NA #> 3  3  6  9 NA NA #> 4 10 13 NA 16 NA #> 5 11 14 NA 17 NA #> 6 12 15 NA 18 NA #> 7 19 22 NA NA 25 #> 8 20 23 NA NA 26 #> 9 21 24 NA NA 27  ## 3. Row-wise reduction ------------------------------------------- m1 <- data.frame(x = c(1, 2, 3), row.names = c("r1", "r2", "r3")) m2 <- data.frame(y = c(4, 5, 6), row.names = c("r1", "r3", "r4"))  result_rows <- run_reduce(m1, m2, check_cols = FALSE) result_rows$data            # rows: r1, r3 (common to both) #>    x y #> r1 1 4 #> r3 3 5  result_rows_union <- run_reduce(m1, m2, check_cols = FALSE,                                 return_common = FALSE) result_rows_union$data      # rows: r1, r2, r3, r4 (NAs where absent) #>     x  y #> r1  1  4 #> r2  2 NA #> r3  3  5 #> r4 NA  6  ## 4. Single data frame (warning, returned as-is) ------------------ result_single <- run_reduce(df1) #> Warning: Only one data frame was supplied. Returning it unchanged (no reduction performed).

what: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
text: Fits regularized regression models — Ridge (alpha = 0), LASSO (alpha = 1), Elastic Net (0 < alpha < 1) — k-fold cross-validation via cv.glmnet. Supports categorical (binary multinomial classification) continuous (Gaussian regression) dependent variables. multiple alpha values supplied, evaluated identical cross-validation folds best-performing model returned alongside comparison table. Unpenalized covariates (e.g., batch indicators, demographic confounders) can included not_penalized argument; coefficients estimated freely predictors remain subject regularization.

title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
code: run_regreg(   x,   metadata,   pred,   ref = NULL,   not_penalized = NULL,   is_numeric = NULL,   train_percent = 0.8,   alpha = 0.5,   lambda = "1se",   cv_folds = 10L,   type_measure = NULL,   parallel = FALSE,   standardize = FALSE,   maxit = 100000L,   seed = 123 )

title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
text: x data.frame, tibble, matrix predictor variables. Must column names (special characters supported). columns treated features penalized unless overridden not_penalized. Rows must correspond samples metadata. metadata data.frame tibble containing sample-level metadata. Must number rows x order. Must contain column specified pred. pred single character string naming column metadata use dependent variable. column numeric, Gaussian regression performed. character factor, classification performed. ref single character string specifying reference category categorical pred. Required classification; ignored numeric pred. not_penalized Optional character vector column names metadata /x include model without penalization (penalty factor = 0). Useful known confounders covariates must controlled. column named pred automatically excluded warning accidentally included (e.g. passing names(metadata)). Default: NULL. is_numeric Optional character vector column names listed not_penalized treated already numeric, bypassing automatic non-numeric detection integer-code conversion. Use column genuinely numeric stored character factor (e.g. due upstream data import). names must exist metadata x must also appear not_penalized. Default: NULL. train_percent numeric scalar (0, 1) specifying proportion samples used model training. remaining 1 - train_percent held evaluation. Default: 0.8. alpha numeric scalar vector values [0, 1]: 0 Ridge regression (L2 penalty; retains predictors) 1 LASSO regression (L1 penalty; performs variable selection) (0, 1) Elastic Net (blend L1 L2) vector supplied values evaluated best model selected. Default: 0.5. lambda single string controlling lambda selection cross-validation: "1se" Lambda within one standard error minimum CV error (conservative; fewer selected predictors). Default. "min" Lambda minimizing CV error (less conservative; selected predictors). cv_folds positive integer 3 20 specifying number cross-validation folds. Default: 10. type_measure character string specifying CV loss function. NULL (default) selects automatically based pred type: Categorical "class" (misclassification error). Also accepts "auc" (binary ; falls back "class" multinomial) "deviance". Numeric "mse" (mean squared error). Also accepts "mae" "deviance". parallel Logical. TRUE, cross-validation parallelized using registered foreach backend (e.g., via doParallel::registerDoParallel()). warning issued TRUE backend registered. Default: FALSE. standardize Logical. Whether standardize predictors inside glmnet prior fitting. Set FALSE (default) data already preprocessed/scaled. Default: FALSE. maxit positive integer specifying maximum number iterations coordinate descent convergence. Default: 100000. seed single numeric value passed set.seed() reproducibility train/test split cross-validation fold assignment. Set NULL disable. Default: 123.

title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
text: named list class "run_regreg" following elements: modelsummary data.frame summarising performance metrics (rank column) every alpha tested. sampledistribution data.frame showing number training testing samples per group (classification) overall (regression), Total row appended. BestModel named list best-performing alpha: Model fitted cv.glmnet object. Performance one-row data.frame metrics (Accuracy + Kappa classification; MSE, RMSE, MAE, R2 regression). Coefficients data.frame non-zero coefficients (intercept included non-zero). Columns: Feature, Coefficient, OddsRatio (classification) just Feature Coefficient (regression). Multinomial models include leading Comparison column. N_Coefficients Integer; total non-zero coefficients including intercept(s). ConfusionMatrix confusionMatrix object (caret); NULL regression. Predictions data.frame columns Actual Predicted. Lambda Numeric; selected lambda value. Alpha Numeric; alpha value model. AllModels named list ("alpha_<value>") structure BestModel every alpha tested. AlphaComparison data.frame comparing tested alpha values performance metric number non-zero coefficients. NULL one alpha tested.

title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
text: Perform Regularized Regression Analysis Data assembly: feature matrix taken x. not_penalized column names belonging metadata provided, columns appended feature matrix. penalty.factor vector length ncol(x) set 1 penalized predictors 0 unpenalized ones, passed glmnet::cv.glmnet. Cross-validation consistency: Fold IDs generated (using seed) reused across tested alpha values, ensuring fair, apple--apple comparison regularization paths. Multinomial coefficients: multinomial models raw coefficient matrix normalized relative reference level comparison reflects log-odds vs. reference. Custom pairwise contrasts supported version; use Coefficients data frame compute differences manually. p-values? P-values provided statistically invalid regularized variable selection. Report cross-validated performance metrics coefficient magnitudes instead.

title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ## ---- Simulate data ------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(42) n  <- 80 p  <- 30  feat <- as.data.frame(   matrix(rnorm(n * p), nrow = n,          dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("feat_", seq_len(p)))) )  meta <- data.frame(   SampleID = paste0("S", seq_len(n)),   Group    = sample(c("Control", "Case"), n, replace = TRUE),   Age      = round(runif(n, 20, 65)) )  ## ---- Binary classification (Elastic Net, single alpha) ------------------- res_bin <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta,   pred          = "Group",   ref           = "Control",   alpha         = 0.5,   train_percent = 0.8,   seed          = 123 ) print(res_bin) res_bin$modelsummary res_bin$BestModel$Coefficients  ## ---- Binary classification (multiple alpha sweep) ------------------------ res_sweep <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta,   pred          = "Group",   ref           = "Control",   alpha         = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1),   lambda        = "1se",   seed          = 123 ) res_sweep$AlphaComparison  ## ---- LASSO with an unpenalized covariate --------------------------------- res_lasso <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta,   pred          = "Group",   ref           = "Control",   not_penalized = "Age",   alpha         = 1,   seed          = 123 )  ## ---- Gaussian regression (numeric pred) ---------------------------------- res_num <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta,   pred          = "Age",   alpha         = c(0, 0.5, 1),   train_percent = 0.75,   seed          = 7 ) res_num$BestModel$Performance  ## ---- Multinomial classification ------------------------------------------ meta3 <- meta meta3$Group <- sample(c("A", "B", "C"), n, replace = TRUE)  res_multi <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta3,   pred          = "Group",   ref           = "A",   alpha         = 0.5,   seed          = 1 ) res_multi$BestModel$Coefficients } # }

what: Recode and Factorize a Column — run_relabel
title: Recode and Factorize a Column — run_relabel
text: function recodes values specified column data frame according provided mapping, converts column factor ordered levels. recoded values appear first factor levels, followed remaining unique values alphabetical order.

title: Recode and Factorize a Column — run_relabel
code: run_relabel(x, column, conversion_map)

title: Recode and Factorize a Column — run_relabel
text: x data frame containing column recoded factorized. column character string specifying name column recode. column must exist data frame. conversion_map named list vector names original values replaced values new values. elements must atomic (character, numeric, logical).

title: Recode and Factorize a Column — run_relabel
text: data frame identical input except specified column recoded according conversion map converted factor ordered levels.

title: Recode and Factorize a Column — run_relabel
text: function performs following steps: Converts specified column character handle mixed data types Applies recoding based conversion map Creates factor levels recoded values first, followed unique values alphabetical order Converts column factor specified level ordering

title: Recode and Factorize a Column — run_relabel
code: # Basic usage with character recoding x <- data.frame(   id = 1:5,   status = c("A", "B", "C", "A", "D") ) map <- c("A" = "Active", "B" = "Inactive", "C" = "Pending") result <- run_relabel(x, "status", map) levels(result$status)  # "Active" "Inactive" "Pending" "D" #> [1] "Active"   "Inactive" "Pending"  "D"         # Usage with numeric to character conversion x2 <- data.frame(   score = c(1, 2, 3, 1, 4),   grade = c(90, 85, 78, 92, 65) ) grade_map <- c("1" = "Excellent", "2" = "Good", "3" = "Fair") result2 <- run_relabel(x2, "score", grade_map)

title: Scale Metabolomics Data — run_scale
text: Applies various scaling methods standardize features prepare data multivariate analysis.

title: Scale Metabolomics Data — run_scale
code: run_scale(x, method = "auto", verbose = TRUE)

title: Scale Metabolomics Data — run_scale
text: x Matrix data frame. Numeric data samples rows features columns. method Character. Scaling method apply. Options: "mean": Mean-centering (centers distribution zero) "auto": Auto-scaling (mean-centering + unit variance scaling) "pareto": Pareto-scaling (mean-centering + sqrt(SD) scaling) verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default: TRUE.

title: Scale Metabolomics Data — run_scale
text: list containing: data Matrix data frame scaled data (class input x) scaling_factors Numeric vector scaling factors applied feature center_values Numeric vector centering values (means) feature method_used Character describing scaling method applied parameters List parameters used

title: Scale Metabolomics Data — run_scale
text: Scaling Methods: mean: Mean-centering . feature centered mean = 0 retains original variance. Useful features already similar scales want preserve relative variance information. auto: Auto-scaling (also called standardization unit variance scaling). feature mean-centered divided standard deviation, resulting mean = 0 SD = 1 features. gives equal weight features regardless original variance. Recommended : Principal Component Analysis (PCA) Hierarchical clustering Correlation analysis t-tests ANOVA univariate multivariate methods Auto-scaling can amplify noise low-variance features. pareto: Pareto-scaling. feature mean-centered divided square root standard deviation. provides compromise scaling auto-scaling, reducing influence large values preserving original variance structure. Recommended : PLS-DA (Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis) OPLS-DA (Orthogonal PLS-DA) sPLS-DA (Sparse PLS-DA) PLS-type methods Pareto-scaling generally preferred discriminant analysis balances importance large small features better auto-scaling. Choosing Scaling Method: choice depends downstream analysis: exploratory data analysis (PCA, clustering): auto-scaling classification/discrimination (PLS-DA): Pareto-scaling feature magnitudes carry meaning: mean-centering

title: Scale Metabolomics Data — run_scale
text: van den Berg, R.., Hoefsloot, H.C., Westerhuis, J.., Smilde, .K., & van der Werf, M.J. (2006). Centering, scaling, transformations: improving biological information content metabolomics data. BMC Genomics, 7, 142. doi:10.1186/1471-2164-7-142 Becker, R.., Chambers, J.M., & Wilks, .R. (1988). New S Language. Wadsworth & Brooks/Cole. doi:10.1201/9781351074988
